query: cytokine levels in pancreatic juice and association with pancreatic diseases

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Pancreas and Acute Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis is an inflammatory disease of the pancreas caused by obstruction of the pancreatic duct, gene mutation, or alcoholism. Acute pancreatitis is among the most common causes of hospitalization in the United States. Inflammation in acute pancreatitis occurs by activating neutrophils and granulocytes, which secrete inflammatory cytokines. NF-κB, Janus kinase-signal transducer and activator of transcription (JAK-STAT), and mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathways play crucial roles in cell activation during pancreatitis. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Pancreas -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Similar to the biliary tree, the pancreatic duct system can also become obstructed. Abnormal ductal architecture or insufficient sphincter function at the level of the duodenum can cause stasis of exocrine secretions. Increasing intraluminal pressure then promotes the activation of digestive proenzymes within the pancreatic ductal system. Parenchymal inflammation ensues, triggering positive-feedback cytokine cascades that further exacerbate pancreatitis. Exocrine enzymes, notably alpha-amylase and lipase, spill over into the circulation. Lipase itself is a sensitive and specific serum marker for diagnosing pancreatitis. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Insufficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic pancreatitis is caused by recurrent episodes of inflammation that eventually progress to fibrosis. Consequently, both the endocrine and exocrine functions of the pancreas can be affected. [4] Proposed mechanisms include the toxic metabolic, oxidative stress, obstructive, and necrosis-fibrosis theories. [21] [23] Typically, when chyme reaches the duodenum, both secretin and cholecystokinin stimulate the secretion of approximately 1.5 L of pancreatic fluid that contains pancreatic enzymes (amylase, lipase, and protease), water, and ions (bicarbonate and phosphate). These enzymes are essential to digestion in the small intestine. [6] In EPI, an insufficient amount of pancreatic enzymes reaches the small intestine.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: These genes are implicated in dysregulated trypsin activation, impaired protective mechanisms, and abnormal ductal secretion, all contributing to pancreatic injury and chronic disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Panniculitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The role of specific cytokines, such as adipokines, immune complexes, and vascular damage that allows for immune complex deposition are also proposed mechanisms for this disease's pathophysiology. [5] These mechanisms may occur concurrently or separately and are still under investigation. There have been reports in the literature of patients with normal levels of pancreatic enzymes still present with pancreatic panniculitis, with biopsy findings confirming the diagnosis. [14] Alternative explanations for this presentation include the involvement of transient lipase and amylase elevations or decreased ability to clear these enzymes in certain disease states or when taking certain medications, such as end-stage kidney disease or the usage of immunomodulators. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Pancreatitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Type 1: IgG4-related pancreatitis, associated with a serum IgG4 concentration greater than twice the normal reference range in most affected individuals. Diagnostic findings include infiltration with greater than 10 IgG4-positive plasma cells per high-power field (HPF), a cartwheel pattern of fibrosis, lymphocytic and plasma cell inflammation of venules, and a histologic description of lymphoplasmacytic sclerosing pancreatitis. The most common clinical presentation of type 1 AIP is painless jaundice with laboratory studies suggesting biliary obstruction and hyperglycemia. Other cases present with abdominal pain or are discovered in asymptomatic individuals with a pancreatic mass, pancreatic duct stricturing, or an enlarged pancreas. Acute pancreatitis is an unusual presentation in type 1 AIP, but it can result in exocrine and endocrine pancreatic insufficiency. [5] The pancreas may be the only organ affected, or there may also be other organs involved with corresponding clinical presentations, including: Bile ducts (secondary sclerosing cholangitis) Retroperitoneal fibrosis Kidneys (tubulointerstitial nephritis) Lungs Submandibular and parotid glands (Sjögren disease) Sublingual glands (sclerosing sialadenitis) Orbits and lacrimal glands (IgG4-related dacryoadenitis and sialoadenitis, formerly Mikulicz disease) Joints (rheumatoid arthritis)

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring BUN and hematocrit levels provides important information about intravascular volume status. Rising values may reflect inadequate fluid resuscitation and correlate with poorer outcomes. [18] C-reactive protein levels can assist in estimating disease severity. Serum immunoglobulin G4 measurement is warranted when autoimmune pancreatitis is suspected. Genetic testing should be considered in patients with early-onset, recurrent, or idiopathic acute pancreatitis, especially if a family history of pancreatic disease has been elicited. Variants associated with increased susceptibility and the proteins they encode include the following: PRSS1 : Cationic trypsinogen SPINK1 : Serine protease inhibitor Kazal-type 1 CFTR : Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator CTRC : Chymotrypsin C CASR : Calcium-sensing receptor CLDN2: Claudin-2, a tight junction protein involved in paracellular ion transport within pancreatic ducts

retrieved_docs.title: Ranson Criteria -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The hematocrit is the 1 laboratory parameter often used to stage acute pancreatitis. Hematocrit greater than 47% on admission has been shown to be a good predictor of pancreatic necrosis. Other markers also used to stage acute pancreatitis include C-reactive protein (CRP) and interleukin-6 levels. Many other biological markers have shown promise in predicting the severity of acute pancreatitis (eg, trypsinogen activation peptide, phospholipase A2, and polymorphonuclear elastase), but not all are better than using CRP.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenocarcinoma(Archived) -- Etiology -- Adenocarcinoma of the Pancreas
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental risk factors contributing to pancreatic adenocarcinoma include smoking, high fasting plasma glucose, high body mass index, western diet, and alcohol consumption, prolonged use of aspirin. [11] Hereditary risk factors also contribute to the risk, with up to 10 percent of individuals with pancreatic cancer having a family history. [12] [13] ABO blood type has been associated with several gastrointestinal cancers, including pancreatic cancer as well as cystic fibrosis, with 2 to 5-fold higher risk in patients who have undergone a lung transplant. [14] [15] [16] [17] [18] The amount of cigarette smoking significantly increases the risk of adenocarcinoma of the pancreas, [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] a population-attributable fraction of deaths due to pancreatic cancer associated with smoking is 11% to 32%. [24] Other important risk factors include H. pylori in gastric adenocarcinoma [25] , gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and alcohol in esophageal adenocarcinoma. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The resulting enzyme activation destroys pancreatic parenchyma and peripancreatic fat necrosis, releasing damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). These DAMPs initiate a robust inflammatory response by stimulating cytokine release and recruiting neutrophils and monocytes. The ensuing inflammatory cascade causes endothelial injury, increased vascular permeability, and microvascular thrombosis, which drive systemic complications such as systemic inflammatory response syndrome and multiple organ dysfunction syndrome. The severity of inflammation ranges from mild, self-limited interstitial edema to extensive necrosis and hemorrhage. In necrotizing pancreatitis, these processes are prolonged and more severe, frequently resulting in superinfection, persistent organ failure, or death.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatitis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis is a response to injury to the pancreas and results in inflammation of the pancreas. Chronic pancreatitis results in chronic changes within the pancreas including a mononuclear infiltrate and fibrosis of the pancreas.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Pancreas -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The reflux seen in APBJ can be pancreaticobiliary or biliopancreatic. Pancreaticobiliary reflux is far more common because pressures in the common bile duct are generally lower than the pancreatic duct, creating a pressure gradient which allows pancreatic enzymes to enter the bile. Reflux can cause gallbladder and biliary amylase levels to rise over 100000 IU/L, driving inflammation that is believed to convey higher risk for biliary tree malignancies, notably gallbladder carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma. [33] Interestingly, the Japanese committee noted higher rates of biliary cancers in APBJ patients without bile duct dilatation than those with dilatation (42.4% vs. 21.6%).

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Insufficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, pancreatic insufficiency often results from conditions that impair acinar cell function, decreasing digestive enzyme production. [24] Chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic resection, cystic fibrosis, and pancreatic cancer lead to loss of pancreatic parenchyma, causing impaired enzyme production. Tumors and ductal stenoses lead to impaired enzyme outflow. Postoperative anatomical changes (such as duodenal resection), inflammatory enteropathies (including celiac disease and inflammatory bowel disease), and somatostatinomas alter the endogenous stimulation of cholecystokinin and enteropeptidase (formerly enterokinase), interfering with the activation of digestive enzymes. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Pancreatitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory Testing: IgG4 serum concentrations are elevated in approximately 50% of type 1 AIP cases and are generally in the normal reference range in types 2 and 3. Cancer antigen 19-9 blood tests are modestly elevated in some patients with AIP, but not to the levels seen in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrin -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: ZES is confirmed by the presence of elevated fasting serum gastrin concentration far more than 100 pg/ml in association with increased basal gastric acid secretion and/or low gastric pH below 2.0. Another useful test to confirm the diagnosis is the secretin stimulation test, potentially resulting in increased gastrin to over 120 pg/ml over basal. [18] Secretin is a peptide hormone made in the S cells of the duodenum in response to acidity and released into the bloodstream to affect multiple organs like the stomach, pancreas, and liver. In the stomach, secretin functions to inhibit the release of gastric acid from the parietal cells. In the pancreas, secretin triggers the release of bicarbonate from the ductal cells. In the liver, it triggers the release of bile, which functions to break down dietary fats. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Secretin -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Secretin plays a role in diagnosing pancreatic insufficiency. Administration of secretin increases pancreatic secretions and causes dilation of pancreatic ducts. Therefore, secretin administration occurs during endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) to aid in cannulization. [16] Recent studies show that secretin-enhanced ERCP is more efficacious in the evaluation of pancreatic secretions, and possible ductal obstruction. [17] Ultimately, secretin-enhanced ERCP is more useful in detecting inflammatory and neoplastic conditions of the pancreas versus the use of conventional methods such as magnetic resonance imaging or computerized tomography.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated with hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم), meaning that patients experiencing an acute inflammatory attack of the pancreas often exhibit low serum chloride levels, which can reflect the fluid and electrolyte disturbances commonly seen in this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with زيادة قلوية الدم والأنسجة (increased alkalinity of the blood and tissues) and are mapped to الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), an inflammatory condition of the pancreas. This connection indicates that non‑diabetic pancreatic disease often presents with systemic alkalosis and corresponds to pancreatitis, emphasizing the need to monitor blood pH and recognize pancreatic inflammation in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated with elevated blood cholesterol levels (فرط كوليسترول الدم) as well as reduced blood chloride concentrations (نقص كلوريد الدم). These biochemical abnormalities can serve as important clues in the evaluation of patients with acute pancreatitis, influencing both diagnostic considerations and therapeutic strategies. Recognizing the link between hypercholesterolemia and hypochloremia in the context of acute pancreatic inflammation helps clinicians monitor metabolic disturbances, tailor supportive care, and potentially address underlying lipid abnormalities to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is situated within the broader category of “other endocrine disorders.” Within this group, several specific conditions are identified as subtypes or manifestations: “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia) represents elevated blood glucose levels; “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” denotes diabetes mellitus without complications; “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia) reflects abnormally low blood glucose and is also recognized as a component of the wider “other endocrine disorders” category; “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia) describes excessive circulating insulin; “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) is a pancreatic tumor that secretes glucagon; and “اضطراب إفراز الجسترين” (gastrin secretion disorder) indicates abnormal gastrin release. The gastrin secretion disorder further leads to “الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ” (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities), illustrating a downstream effect on the gastric mucosa. Together, these relationships delineate how diabetes‑related endocrine pancreatic diseases encompass a spectrum of hyper‑ and hypoglycemic states, hormone‑excess conditions, neoplastic processes, and secondary gastrointestinal disturbances, all nested within the larger framework of endocrine disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1302
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders that are not classified as diabetes are clinically associated with an increase in the alkalinity of blood and tissues (زيادة قلوية الدم والأنسجة), indicating that non‑diabetic pancreatic disease can contribute to metabolic conditions characterized by elevated pH in the circulatory system and surrounding tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2046
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES encompasses a spectrum of related conditions. It includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which denotes elevated blood glucose levels, and مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, representing diabetes without complications. Neurological involvement is reflected by اعتلال عصبي سكري, a diabetic neuropathy. Facial manifestations may appear as ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive hemi‑facial atrophy that can be associated with pancreatic endocrine disorders. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by فرط أنسولين الدم, an excess of circulating insulin, and by ورم غلوكاغوني, a glucagon‑producing tumor of the pancreas. Together these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations and complications that arise from diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma hyperosmolarity (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم) and is a common manifestation in both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2). It is also linked to insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين) and can coexist with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Dehydration (التجفاف) is mapped to plasma hyperosmolarity, indicating that fluid loss often precipitates the hyperosmolar state, and dehydration itself is clinically associated with acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney failure is further clinically associated with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), dehydration, abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), and low blood volume. These interconnections highlight that disturbances in fluid balance and severe hyperglycemia can lead to plasma hyperosmolarity, which together with diabetes‑related metabolic stress may precipitate acute kidney failure, often accompanied by hypovolemia, mental status changes, and pancreatic inflammation. Recognizing these linked clinical features is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients presenting with hyperosmolar, diabetic, or renal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a classification that encompasses several distinct disorders of pancreatic endocrine function. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes mellitus without any associated complications and represents the basic metabolic disturbance of chronic hyperglycemia. It also comprises اعتلال عصبي سكري, a chronic diabetic neuropathy in which prolonged hyperglycemia damages peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. The category further lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive atrophy of one side of the facial tissues that can be observed in some patients with endocrine pancreatic disease. فرط أنسولين الدم is another child condition, describing an excess of circulating insulin that may arise from insulin‑producing tumors (insulinoma) or other dysregulation of pancreatic β‑cell activity. Finally, ورم غلوكاغوني refers to a glucagon‑secreting pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma) that causes hyperglycemia and a characteristic clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of pancreatic endocrine disorders—from uncomplicated diabetes to its neuropathic complications, hormonal excess states, and neoplastic growths—underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol frequently co‑occurs with this form of diabetes. In addition, الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ (acute pancreatitis) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia), suggesting that high cholesterol levels may also be linked to the development of severe pancreatic inflammation. Together, these connections imply that hypercholesterolemia can serve as a common clinical link between type 2 diabetes and acute pancreatitis, underscoring the importance of lipid monitoring and management in diabetic patients to potentially mitigate the risk of pancreatitis and its complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1589
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated both with hypercholesterolemia and with chloride deficiency (نقص كلوريد الدم). These relationships indicate that patients with type 2 diabetes often have elevated cholesterol levels, which can increase the risk of developing acute pancreatitis, and that the inflammatory episode of acute pancreatitis may be accompanied by electrolyte disturbances such as low serum chloride. Recognizing the links among type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, acute pancreatitis, and chloride deficiency supports more comprehensive evaluation and integrated management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), indicating that patients with non‑diabetic pancreatic conditions often present with abdominal pain as a key clinical feature.
